narration.default: So far your conditions have been simple — one comparison at a time. But real-world rules are often "BOTH this AND that" or "EITHER this OR that". Scratch had three green hexagon blocks for combining conditions: "and", "or", and "not". C uses SYMBOLS instead: && (and), || (or), ! (not). Same job, shorter to type. Today you'll see all three on a movie-theater example: must be 13+ AND have a ticket; under 5 OR over 65 = free; if NOT have a ticket, buy one first.

Lines 6 to 8 use && which means AND: if (age >= 13 && hasTicket). For the IF to fire, BOTH halves must be true. age is 15, so age >= 13 is true. hasTicket is 1, which counts as true (in C, 0 means false; anything else means true). Both true → the print runs and "You can enter." appears. If EITHER half were false, the print would be skipped. Use && when you need EVERY rule to be satisfied at once. Read && out loud as "and" — it makes the code easier to follow.

Lines 9 to 11 use || which means OR: if (age < 5 || age > 65). For the IF to fire, AT LEAST ONE half must be true. age is 15. age < 5? No. age > 65? No. Both false → the print is SKIPPED, "Free entry." does not appear. Try changing age to 3 — then age < 5 is true, so the OR is true, and the print fires. The two pipes || are typed by holding Shift and pressing the \ key (above Enter on most keyboards). Read || out loud as "or".

Lines 12 to 14 use ! which means NOT — it FLIPS true to false and false to true: if (!hasTicket). hasTicket is 1 (true), so !hasTicket becomes false → the print is skipped. If you change hasTicket to 0 (no ticket), then !hasTicket becomes true → the print fires. Use ! when you want to act on the OPPOSITE of a condition. A common pattern: if (!isLoggedIn) { ask them to log in; }. Read ! as "not" — !x is "not x".

Trace each line in your head before clicking. age=15, hasTicket=1. (1) && line: 15 >= 13 true AND 1 is true → print runs ("You can enter."). (2) || line: 15 < 5 false OR 15 > 65 false → both false → silent. (3) ! line: !1 is false → silent. So only ONE line should print. Click Run to confirm. Now change hasTicket to 0 and Run again — different lines fire. Try age = 3 with hasTicket = 1, then age = 70 with hasTicket = 1. Each combination triggers a different mix of branches.
